data.title: NCino outlines fiscal 2027 free cash flow of $132M-$137M while targeting 10% ACV growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: nCino (NCNO) Q4 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • “We exceeded our financial guidance across every key metric and delivered an exceptional ACV result, up 17% year-over-year,” with CEO Sean Desmond framing the outperformance as “largely driven by customers embracing our AI strategy and product innovation” (CEO, President & Director Sean Desmond).
  • AI commercialization and adoption was a core theme, with Desmond stating that “over 170 customers…have already purchased AI intelligence units as of the end of fiscal '26,” and adding, “we believe nCino is rapidly becoming the de facto AI platform for financial institutions across the globe” (CEO Desmond).
  • International and enterprise momentum were highlighted through deal callouts, including: “in the U.S. enterprise market, we delivered our best sales quarter in over 4 years,” a “marquee net new customer win in Austria,” and “the fourth quarter signing of one of the largest banks in the world for a commercial lending transformation” in Japan (CEO Desmond).
  • A key go-to-market change was announced: “nCino has hired Keith Kettell as our new Chief Revenue Officer,” as Desmond said the company is “further investing in our go-to-market organization” (CEO Desmond).
  • CFO Greg Orenstein reported Q4 financials and profitability expansion, stating, “Total revenues were $149.7 million,” “subscription revenues were $133.4 million,” and “non-GAAP operating income…was $34.7 million or 23% of total revenues” (CFO & Treasurer Gregory D. Orenstein).

Outlook

  • Q1 fiscal 2027 guidance was set at “total revenues of $154.5 million to $156.5 million” and “subscription revenues of $137 million to $139 million,” with “non-GAAP operating income…approximately $38 million to $40 million” (CFO Orenstein).
  • Based on the provided estimates, Q1 revenue guidance of $154.5 million to $156.5 million vs. $152,743,800.0 (analysts’ estimate). nCino did not provide EPS guidance in the current transcript, so no EPS comparison was included.
  • For fiscal 2027, nCino guided to “total revenues of $639 million to $643 million” and “subscription revenues of $569 million to $573 million,” and said, “Excluding U.S. mortgage, our guidance assumes 10% to 11% year-over-year subscription revenues growth” (CFO Orenstein).
  • On bookings/ACV, management guided to “net additions of $60 million to $65 million on a constant currency and organic basis,” implying “ending ACV to $662.5 million to $667.5 million, representing 10% ACV growth at the midpoint” (CFO Orenstein).
  • A guidance framework change was disclosed: “effective for fiscal '27, we will be providing annual guidance for free cash flow in lieu of quarterly and annual guidance for non-GAAP net income attributable to nCino per share” (CFO Orenstein).

Financial Results

  • Q4 results included “Total revenues…$149.7 million,” with “subscription revenues…$133.4 million” and “professional services revenues…$16.3 million” (CFO Orenstein).
  • Profitability and earnings were described as: “Non-GAAP net income attributable to nCino…was $42.8 million or $0.37 per diluted share,” and “free cash flow was $12.5 million” (CFO Orenstein).
  • Balance sheet and customer metrics included “cash and cash equivalents of $88.7 million,” “ACV as of January 31, 2026, was $602.4 million,” and “ACV net retention rate…increased to 112%” (CFO Orenstein).
  • Capital return and financing actions were emphasized: “We repurchased approximately 1 million shares…for total consideration of $25 million,” and “today we announced a $100 million accelerated share repurchase program,” alongside “a $200 million term loan expansion of our existing credit facility…funded by some of our largest customers” (CFO Orenstein).

Q&A

  • Alexander Sklar, Raymond James: Asked about close rates/win rates and pipeline coverage; CEO Desmond said “conversion rates were healthy, but we just had a larger pipeline,” and cited “discipline of just pipeline management overall” (CEO Desmond).
  • Alexander Sklar, Raymond James: Asked about where AI usage is strongest and credit allotments; CEO Desmond cited “agentic credit reviews,” “Locate and file,” and “credit monitoring,” and said nCino sold “large enough blocks of intelligence units upfront” to avoid customers feeling “nickel and dimed” (CEO Desmond).
  • Joseph Vruwink, Baird: Asked if AI orchestration threatens differentiation; CEO Desmond said “deploying that code…in a highly regulated industry…is still hard,” adding nCino is focused on “an Agentic operating system future” (CEO Desmond).
  • Michael Infante, Morgan Stanley: Asked about pricing transition and renewal pushback; CEO Desmond said nCino is “exceeding our internal plans and targets,” and that customers are “more focused on…outcomes…versus the old per user per seat model” (CEO Desmond).
  • Cristopher Kennedy, William Blair: Asked for ACV by category; CFO Orenstein replied, “we don't have that for this call” (CFO Orenstein).
  • Aaron Kimson, Citizens: Asked about CRO timing and sales execution; CEO Desmond said Paul Clarkson “is stepping aside for personal reasons” and that the CRO role helps nCino “scale to $1 billion and beyond” (CEO Desmond).
  • Charles Nabhan, Stephens: Asked about Sandbox and DocFox; CEO Desmond said Sandbox is “the foundation…of our integration gateway,” while DocFox is tied to “complex commercial onboarding,” with pipeline the company expects to “convert…in the first half of this fiscal year” (CEO Desmond).

Sentiment Analysis

  • “Slightly negative” to “neutral” analyst tone, with repeated probing on AI disruption risk, pricing pushback, and reconciling ACV-to-revenue mechanics, including questions on whether AI can “eat into nCino's differentiation” (Joseph Vruwink, Baird: Question) and why subscription revenue guidance does not align with ending ACV (George McGrehan on for Koji Ikeda, BofA: Question).
  • “Positive” management tone in prepared remarks, leaning into confidence language such as “we feel extremely confident about our position” and “we feel really good about where we are,” while responses to moat questions were assertive: “we believe our data moat is unparalleled and unique” (CEO Desmond).
  • Compared with Q3, management’s tone in Q4 added more emphasis on capital return and financing actions (ASR and term-loan expansion), while continuing the Q3 narrative that AI is creating a “halo effect” and driving “early renewals” (CEO Desmond; CFO Orenstein).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • AI adoption metrics increased from Q3’s “over 110 customers that have now purchased Banking Advisor intelligence units” to Q4’s “over 170 customers…having already purchased AI intelligence units,” alongside usage acceleration described as “banking adviser usage up over 25x in March compared to usage in October” (CEO Desmond).
  • Platform pricing penetration rose from Q3’s “approximately 27% of our ACV” to Q4’s “approximately 38% of our ACV away from seat-based pricing to platform pricing” (CEO Desmond; CFO Orenstein).
  • Q4 introduced a senior leadership change (new CRO) that was not present in Q3, and added a new reporting emphasis: fiscal 2027 free cash flow guidance replacing quarterly and EPS guidance (CEO Desmond; CFO Orenstein).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management directly addressed the market narrative that “AI will replace SaaS,” arguing banking is different due to “regulatory compliance,” “guardrails,” and the need for systems that are “traceable and auditable” (CEO Desmond).
  • On monetization pacing, CFO Orenstein cautioned that “financial institutions are going to adopt AI at a very deliberate pace,” and said fiscal 2027 guidance “does not yet contemplate” incremental bundles of intelligence units (CFO Orenstein).
  • Guidance risk language centered on deal timing variability: “these large deals can be inherently difficult to predict in both their timing and eventual sizing” (CFO Orenstein).

Final Takeaway

Management described fiscal 2026 as a year of improved bookings and retention tied to AI adoption, highlighted a move to platform pricing, and paired that narrative with material capital allocation actions, including a newly announced $100 million accelerated share repurchase and a $200 million term loan expansion. For fiscal 2027, leadership guided to $132 million to $137 million of free cash flow, 10% ending ACV growth at the midpoint, and reiterated a focus on reaccelerating subscription revenue growth while managing AI monetization expectations given “deliberate” customer adoption.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: TruBridge expects modest 2026 revenue growth while anticipating 200 bps adjusted EBITDA margin improvement amid strategic review
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TruBridge (TBRG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Christopher Fowler framed Q4 and year-end results alongside two governance items, saying the company filed its 10-K after finding “certain out-of-period adjustments during final audit procedures with our new external auditor,” emphasizing “these adjustments are noncash and not material to our fiscal 2025 financial statements or to our previously issued financials,” and adding that TruBridge has been “engaged in a strategic review process considering a range of alternatives to maximize shareholder value.”

  • CEO Fowler said the strategic review is driving a change in disclosure posture: “As a result, we are not issuing formal guidance today, but we expect to achieve modest revenue growth in 2026 and anticipate approximately 200 basis points of improvement in adjusted EBITDA margins.”

  • CEO Fowler highlighted commercial momentum and mix, pointing to “bookings of $19.8 million on a total contract value basis,” support from “growing SaaS,” and “strategic partners, including Microsoft and our exclusive Dragon Copilot integration with TruBridge EHR,” while also calling out pipeline changes: “the dollar value of our overall sales pipeline is currently the highest it has been in 9 quarters and has increased 53% since the beginning of Q3.”

  • CFO Vinay Bassi said the audit work led to revisions: “we identified some material noncash misstatements primarily related to the timing of revenue for some products and associated contract costs, capitalized software and certain other nonroutine items,” adding, “We have revised these prior period financials to reflect them in the appropriate period.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated an informal 2026 framework rather than issuing guidance, with CEO Fowler stating, “we expect to achieve modest revenue growth in 2026 and anticipate approximately 200 basis points of improvement in adjusted EBITDA margins.”

  • CFO Bassi repeated the same directional outlook: “while we are not providing formal guidance due to our strategic review process, we remain confident that we can achieve modest revenue growth in 2026, along with continued adjusted EBITDA margin expansion of approximately 200 basis points.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter, TruBridge moved from providing quarterly and full-year 2025 guidance plus an early 2026 margin expansion view to withholding formal guidance entirely; in Q3, CFO Bassi said, “we will not provide formal 2026 guidance until early next year as usual,” while also stating TruBridge believed it would deliver “margin expansion of around 200 basis points from the midpoint of our full year 2025 guidance.”

  • The analysts estimates provided were for fiscal 2026 quarters, but management did not provide EPS or revenue guidance figures for 2026 in the Q4 transcript, so no guidance-vs.-estimates comparison was available.

Financial Results

  • TruBridge reported Q4 revenue of $87.2 million and adjusted EBITDA of $19.2 million, with CEO Fowler stating revenue was “in line with the midpoint of the revised guidance we provided last quarter,” and CFO Bassi adding revenue was “a decrease of approximately 1% compared to a year ago,” while adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to “22% margin this quarter.”

  • Full-year 2025 results included revenue of $346.8 million and adjusted EBITDA of $68.7 million, with CEO Fowler adding free cash flow was $20 million and CFO Bassi stating cash flow from operations was $37 million.

  • Segment detail in Q4 showed Financial Health revenue of $56.2 million and Patient Care revenue of $31 million; CFO Bassi attributed Financial Health performance to “strong growth in the Encoder business,” while Patient Care reflected “a 6.6% year-over-year decline, primarily due to the sunset of our Centriq.”

  • Balance sheet and liquidity updates included CFO Bassi’s statement that TruBridge ended the quarter with $24.9 million in cash and reduced net debt to “approximately $139.8 million,” with “net leverage…2x,” alongside a financing update: “in November 2025, we entered into an amended and restated credit agreement…expires in 2030 with up to $250 million in credit facilities.”

Q&A

  • Sean Dodge, BMO Capital Markets: asked how to use the new ACV metric to assess visibility and later pressed on strategic review timing; CFO Bassi replied, “the recurring revenues and some assumption of bookings conversion…into revenue and attrition…is the right way of doing it,” while CEO Fowler said on timing, “we do not have a time line on this…more about getting to the right outcome as it is about hitting a certain deadline.”

  • Maxi Ma, Deutsche Bank: asked about bookings volatility, conversion timing, and capacity constraints; CEO Fowler said, “There are no capacity constraints,” and described booking timing as “a little bit at the mercy of the customer,” tied to replacement contract terms and facility onboarding dynamics.

  • Maxi Ma, Deutsche Bank: asked whether margin expansion is nearing a ceiling; CEO Fowler said, “I don't think we're at the end when we hit that 200. And I don't know where the ceiling is,” and CFO Bassi added, “revenue mix…will play as technology solutions like encoder keeps picking up.”

  • Jeffrey Garro, Stephens: asked about synergy from having Patient Care and Financial Health together and visibility into 2026; CEO Fowler pointed to “interconnectivity…with the rural community customer base with the EHR,” and CFO Bassi said, “We will continue to have some seasonality of some of the revenue streams and the timing of bookings.”

  • Sarah James, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked for detail and KPIs on AI to predict denials; CEO Fowler said TruBridge is “in a pilot format,” “still in a learning phase,” but outlined a target area: “probably 80%, 85% of claims are going through…So now there's an opportunity for 15% of the claims to be improved.”

  • Ryan Halsted, RBC Capital Markets: asked about a rural health fund and competitive dynamics; CEO Fowler said TruBridge is “100% locked in on helping our hospitals get into that $50 billion fund,” noted TruBridge was “selected by SAIC to be their preferred partner for the EHR and RCM technology,” and added on competition, “We have not seen that yet.”

  • Eugene Mannheimer, Prime Executions: asked about the RevSpring partnership impact timing; CEO Fowler said, “I don't think there's a meaningful impact this year,” adding, “we'll start to see some traction there towards the back half of the year and then moving into 2027.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, focusing on visibility (ACV-to-revenue translation), retention/attrition, conversion timing, margin durability, and strategic review timing; the most direct pressure point was the timeline question, while management repeatedly emphasized limits on disclosure.

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks around pipeline, AI initiatives, and balance sheet progress, while sounding cautious on predictability and strategic review timing; CEO Fowler said, “we do not have a time line,” and described pipeline conversion as potentially “a quarter or 2 away from consistent quarterly performance.”

  • Versus the prior quarter, management added a more constrained posture on forward-looking detail due to the strategic review (no formal guidance), while continuing the confidence language on margins; Q3 included explicit 2025 guidance ranges and an early 2026 margin expansion view, whereas Q4 emphasized process, controls, and strategic alternatives.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • TruBridge shifted from issuing quarterly and full-year guidance in Q3 to withholding formal guidance in Q4, with CEO Fowler explicitly tying that change to the strategic review.

  • Q4 introduced two new stock-sensitive governance items absent from Q3’s framing: out-of-period adjustments tied to the first year with a new external auditor, and an ongoing strategic review “considering a range of alternatives to maximize shareholder value.”

  • Commercial narrative moved from Q3’s bookings disappointment and early-Q4 “strong start” commentary to Q4’s larger pipeline and mix assertions, including that the pipeline is “the highest…in 9 quarters,” that larger facilities increased to “30% of the pipeline,” and that “greater than 70%” of pipeline is recurring deals.

  • Analyst focus broadened in Q4, adding strategic review timing and the rural health fund opportunity, while continuing prior-quarter themes of bookings conversion and margin expansion drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged financial reporting and controls work as a near-term issue, with CEO Fowler noting “out-of-period adjustments” and CFO Bassi describing “material noncash misstatements,” while both emphasized they were noncash and tied to strengthening controls.

  • Customer retention remained a stated headwind, with CEO Fowler saying a “decrease in retention in our CBO customers” occurred as global teams adjusted, and that TruBridge is monitoring transition metrics such as “the cash in the door” and “communication with the customers.”

  • Management described uncertainty in the timing of bookings-to-revenue conversion as customer-driven; CEO Fowler said bookings were “at the mercy of the customer” due to replacement contract terms and operational onboarding factors.

Final Takeaway

TruBridge’s Q4 narrative centered on three investor-sensitive messages from management: the completion of a delayed 10-K after noncash revisions tied to a new auditor, the launch of a strategic review that is limiting formal guidance, and an operational plan built around modest 2026 revenue growth with about 200 basis points of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion. Management emphasized pipeline expansion, higher recurring mix, encoder momentum, and balance sheet progress, while acknowledging retention and customer-timed conversion dynamics as ongoing constraints on near-term consistency.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wednesday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
7:00 AM MBA Mortgage Applications

The Mortgage Bankers' Association compiles various mortgage loan indexes. The purchase applications index measures applications at mortgage lenders.

8:15 AM ADP Employment Report

The national employment report from Automated Data Processing Inc. is computed from ADP payroll data and offers advance indications on the U.S. workforce.

The weekly ADP report has been showing jobs up 10K a week on average in March, so that means about 40K a month.

8:30 AM Retail Sales

Retail sales measure the total receipts at stores that sell merchandise and related services to final consumers. Sales are by retail and food services stores.

The consensus looks for retail sales up 0.4 percent in February, with sales ex-autos up 0. percent.

9:05 AM Alberto Musalem Speaks 

St. Louis Federal Reserve Bank President Alberto Musalem speaks and participates in a moderated conversation on the U.S. economy and monetary policy before the American Enterprise Institute.

9:10 AM Michael Barr Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Michael Barr speaks on "AI and Consumer Issues" at the National Fair Housing Alliance 2026 Responsible AI Symposium.

9:45 AM PMI Manufacturing Final

Based on monthly questionnaire surveys of selected companies, the Purchasing Managers' Manufacturing Index (PMI) offers an advance indication of month-to-month activity in the private sector economy by tracking changes in variables such as production, new orders, stock levels, employment, and prices across manufacturing industries. 

No revision expected from the flash at 52.4.

10:00 AM ISM Manufacturing Index

The manufacturing composite index from the Institute for Supply Management is a diffusion index calculated from five of the eleven sub-components of a monthly survey of purchasing managers at roughly 300 manufacturing firms nationwide.

The consensus sees the index pretty steady at 52.3 in March vs. 52.4 in February, suggesting modest growth.

10:00 AM Business Inventories

Business inventories are the dollar amount of inventories held by manufacturers, wholesalers, and retailers. 

10:30 AM EIA Petroleum Status Report

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on petroleum inventories in the U.S., whether produced here or abroad.

data.title: TOMI projects $12M 2026 revenue as it targets recurring orders and expands a $20M iHP opportunity book
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TOMI Environmental Solutions (TOMZ) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Halden Shane framed 2025 as a transition year, saying, "While 2025 was a year that tested our patience, it was also a year that validated our technology, deepened our customer relationships and positioned us to execute on a meaningful revenue opportunity in 2026 and beyond." He highlighted commercialization progress in automation, including that SIS "achieved its first commercial installation at a leading CDMO in June of 2025" and ended the year with "4 fully operational SIS enclosure installations," plus a "signed purchase contract of $500,000" with "a global biopharmaceutical leader" for sterile manufacturing integration.
  • CEO Shane pointed to regulatory and geographic expansion as a catalyst for international growth, stating, "we're finally receiving approvals from the HSE and the BPR submissions" and that TOMI is "now officially recognized in the United Kingdom... as well as in the Netherlands," adding, "The recent approval in the Netherlands strengthens our confidence that other EU countries will soon follow suit."
  • CFO David Vanston said, "Revenue for the year ended December 31, 2025, was $5.6 million compared to $7.7 million in '24," and attributed the decline primarily to "the timing of customer equipment purchases." He added, "Gross margin improved to approximately 55%, up from 46% in '24," and noted, "The net loss was $3.7 million or $0.19 per share, an improvement from a net loss of $4.5 million or $0.22 per share in 2024."
  • COO Elissa Shane emphasized a shift toward recurring orders: "we are actively focusing on open BIT Solution orders and annual service offerings," and said support services and solution demand were building, with "an increase of 16% in the backlog of orders for TOMI Support Services and an increase of 24% in BIT Solution backlog of orders for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same period last year."

Outlook

  • CEO Shane provided a revenue target for 2026, stating, "We estimate our revenue for this year will be around $12 million, bearing any unknowns," and described the pipeline as "the strongest we've ever had" with an "opportunity book for integration projects" at "$16 million" and "the entire SteraMist iHP opportunity book" at "$20 million."
  • COO Shane outlined near-term conversion expectations, saying, "We anticipate approximately $3 million in sales among these 5 customers" and added, "To date, approximately $800,000 has been recognized from the November 2025 $3 million integration pipeline," coming from "10 distinct customer orders," with "final approvals" pending from "the remaining 3 accounts." She also said TOMI "added 4 more integration projects moving towards CapEx approvals" expected to contribute "another $2.3 million" to the near-term pipeline.
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management’s current-quarter outlook included a specific annual revenue estimate ("around $12 million") and more detail on near-term pipeline conversion (recognized amount and approval status), while the prior-quarter discussion focused on backlog build and pending integration contracts expected to close before year-end.

Financial Results

  • CFO Vanston reported 2025 operating and liquidity metrics, stating, "Total operating expenses were $6.9 million, down approximately 10% from '24," and that TOMI ended 2025 with "approximately $88,000 in cash" and "working capital was approximately $1 million." He added, "we used $1.2 million in operating cash during '25."
  • CFO Vanston detailed financing actions tied to liquidity, including "a $535,000 convertible note raise during '25," "a $20 million equity line of credit with Hudson Global Ventures entered into November '25," and "our first draw approximately $94,000 in February '26." He also said, "We've done an effective S3 shelf registration for up to $50 million," and TOMI "engaged Bancroft Capital to explore additional financing options."
  • The analysts estimates JSON was not used for comparison because it did not provide a valid quarter format (it used numeric quarters rather than Q1-Q4), and the transcript did not disclose Q4 2025 quarterly revenue or quarterly EPS figures.

Q&A

  • Carl Wright, Lonetown Capital: asked for "more insight into the global opportunities"; COO Elissa Shane tied near-term EU expansion to registrations, saying, "we expect many other EU states to follow suit within the next upcoming months" and added TOMI has distributors "from Poland, Germany, Netherlands" positioned to act once registrations arrive.
  • Carl Wright, Lonetown Capital: asked how to think about operating expenses; CFO Vanston said, "we do not expect to see a significant jump in our operating expenses" and suggested modeling OpEx "as a percentage of revenue growth" that "would slightly decrease as we grow as a percentage."
  • John Nelson, private investor: asked whether deferred CapEx customers are returning; CEO Halden Shane responded, "We are." Nelson also asked for BIT Solution comparisons; COO Shane said, "There's an increase in the solution between '24 and '25, and we're seeing that continue into '26," tying it to support services and "auto shipment" goals.
  • John Nelson, private investor: asked about cannabis and EPA labeling; CEO Shane said the use case is "for powdery mildew funguses on the plants" and COO Shane added there is "a handful of customers" with early scaling via "handheld treatments" while awaiting "-4 label" approval.
  • John Nelson, private investor: asked about military applications; CEO Shane said TOMI is "a little too small and overwhelmed" to pursue it fully now but added, "I think this is something that we're going to go look into immediately," while COO Shane noted TOMI has submitted RFIs for "military vehicles."
  • John Nelson, private investor: asked about scaling if orders accelerate; CEO Shane said, "we probably need to raise some capital to scale up significantly," and added the current "small team of 20" handled COVID-era volume but the company would need to "increase the size of the company going forward in lots of divisions."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts/investors sentiment was slightly positive and primarily inquisitive, with questions centered on global expansion, operating leverage, deferred CapEx reactivation, and potential new verticals (military, cannabis), including supportive framing such as "congratulations on bringing down operating expenses by 10%."
  • Management sentiment in prepared remarks was positive and assertive around strategy and pipeline depth, including CEO Shane’s statements that the pipeline is "the strongest we've ever had" and that TOMI is "entering 2026 with operational momentum." In Q&A, management was generally direct; on scale-up, CEO Shane acknowledged constraint and financing need: "we probably need to raise some capital."
  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call’s tone included more pointed language from CEO Shane directed at critics ("To the handful of naysayers...") while also providing more granular pipeline conversion and 2026 revenue framing; the prior-quarter tone was more conventionally upbeat about a "turning point" and momentum.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current call shifted emphasis from Q3’s "sales order backlog" and pending integration contract timing to a broader full-year performance review, liquidity actions, and explicit 2026 revenue targeting ("around $12 million") alongside larger stated opportunity books ("$16 million" integration and "$20 million" iHP).
  • Management repeated the strategic arc toward recurring revenue, but Q4 added more operational detail on open orders and service/solution backlog expansion, while Q3 emphasized sequential revenue acceleration, backlog growth, and sector-specific market openings like food safety.
  • Analyst focus moved from Q3’s institutional questions about food safety go-to-market and international execution to Q4’s more concentrated discussion on EU registrations and operating expense trajectory; Q4’s Q&A also featured more investor-driven questions around deferred CapEx and scaling capacity.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Shane cited macro and procurement headwinds affecting customer purchasing decisions, referencing "tariffs, working capital and recently the war," and described customer deferrals tied to "political uncertainty" and "DOGE." He positioned these as temporary, stating, "I can assure it's all temporary."
  • CFO Vanston highlighted liquidity pressure, reporting year-end cash of "approximately $88,000" and outlining multiple financing tools (convertible note, equity line, S-3 shelf) as mitigation steps.
  • COO Shane noted regulatory dependencies in food safety and international expansion, including that "Nestle's pending expansion waits for other international registrations outside the ones received in the European Union" and that "there remains much work to be done on the regulatory front."

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that 2025 results reflected delayed equipment purchases but improved gross margin and reduced operating expenses, while positioning 2026 around pipeline conversion, expanded regulatory access in the U.K. and Netherlands, and a shift toward recurring revenue via open BIT Solution orders and support services. Leadership tied near-term execution to converting integration projects under approval, highlighted liquidity actions to fund scaling, and set a 2026 revenue expectation of around $12 million alongside stated opportunity books of $16 million for integration projects and $20 million for iHP.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Aqua Metals outlines 10,000 to 60,000 metric tons ARC facility range while advancing Lion Energy deal
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Aqua Metals (AQMS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stephen Cotton said 2025 included “deliberate adjustments to our commercialization approach,” including a decision to “simplify the first commercial plant around 2 core feedstock streams, NMC black mass and LFP black mass,” with “3 primary outputs: battery-grade lithium carbonate, nickel, cobalt mixed hydroxide precipitate or MHP, and iron phosphate.” Cotton said the configuration is expected to “reduce execution risk, shorten time to market, lower upfront capital requirements and support attractive unit economics and a stronger payback profile.”

  • Cotton highlighted product quality and partner evaluation activity: “Our lithium carbonate achieved fluorine levels under 30 parts per million,” adding that material at that threshold “has been produced at meaningful scale and distributed to strategic counterparties for evaluation.”

  • Cotton emphasized LFP processing as a key milestone: “We moved from engineering analysis and bench scale work ... through to processing an entire metric ton of LFP cathode scrap at our pilot facility,” and said the lithium carbonate was “validated by OEM and third-party testing.”

  • Cotton described commercial plant sizing flexibility: “we advanced our ARC facility design to support a processing range of 10,000 to 60,000 metric tons of black mass input feedstock annually,” and said the company is conducting “structured due diligence on several candidate sites,” with an expectation to proceed with “final site selection later this year as the remaining commercial conditions come together.”

  • Cotton said the company will not start construction until prerequisites are in place: “what ready means is contracted feedstock, committed offtake and project financing that is genuinely bankable,” adding, “build once, build right.”

  • Cotton said the company “remain[s] actively engaged in diligence with Lion Energy around a transaction structure,” and if completed it would provide “immediate commercial revenue,” expand into “branded energy storage systems,” and provide “a meaningful equity interest in American Battery Factory, or ABF.”

  • CFO Eric West said, “We ended the year with cash and cash equivalents of approximately $10.8 million,” and added, “We ended the year with no long-term debt.” West said 2025 capital raised was “approximately $20 million,” and that net cash used in operating activities was “approximately $10.3 million.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide explicit quantitative revenue or EPS guidance in the transcript.

  • Cotton said 2026 priorities include “advancing engineering and permitting work to support site selection,” “deepening commercial negotiations with supply, offtake and project financing partners,” and moving “partner qualifications for our lithium carbonate and MHP forward.”

  • On the build timeline and gating items, Cotton said the company’s posture is “not going to build before we are ready,” defining readiness as “contracted feedstock, committed offtake and project financing that is genuinely bankable.”

  • On site process timing, Cotton said the company’s goal is to “settle on and secure the lead site and then spend the balance of the year making real progress on site-specific FEL2 engineering.”

Financial Results

  • West reported full-year 2025 operating expense of “approximately $23.3 million” (vs. “approximately $23.8 million” in 2024) and said 2025 included “approximately $9.1 million of impairment and loss on the disposal charges” (vs. “approximately $3.1 million” in 2024), describing these impairment charges as “nonroutine and noncash in nature.”

  • West reported full-year 2025 G&A of “approximately $10.5 million” (vs. “approximately $12 million” in 2024) and full-year 2025 R&D expense of “approximately $1.3 million.”

  • West reported full-year 2025 net loss of “approximately $22.6 million or negative $15.15 per basic and diluted share,” and for Q4 specifically a net loss of “approximately $4.4 million or negative $2.97 per share.”

  • West said operating cash usage improved year-over-year: “Net cash used in operating activities for the full year 2025 was approximately $10.3 million compared to approximately $13.6 million in 2024.”

  • West said the company provided “approximately $2.1 million of short-term financing to Lion Energy,” and that after year-end it “contributed the outstanding note along with an additional $2 million to acquire a subordinated position interest in its senior secured credit facility” while evaluating a potential acquisition.

Q&A

  • Michael Frederick Legg, Jr., The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: asked about “main areas of focus near term and ... synergy” if the Lion Energy acquisition closes; CEO Cotton said the company has been “very deep in due diligence,” and described a combined strategy where “an integrated battery materials and battery energy storage company is much stronger than those that stand on their own,” adding it could “expose the shareholder ... to the optionality” of a combination of “energy storage, battery materials and GigaFactory production.”

  • Legg, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: asked how ABF equity fits; CEO Cotton said “the equity stake in American Battery Factory is a huge value creator,” referenced ABF’s “first GigaFactory in Tucson, Arizona,” and said the company sees “synergistic opportunities as one of the sites we're considering to deploy our ARC facility.”

  • Submitted question (online): requested a site selection update and timing; CEO Cotton said the company is in “active due diligence on 2 specific potential sites” and aims to progress FEL2 engineering after securing a lead site.

  • Submitted question (online): asked about feedstock market volatility; CEO Cotton said “effectively all of the black mass produced in the United States ... is being exported offshore,” and noted projects slowed when lithium carbonate “fell to around $8,000 a ton in 2024,” while pricing “recovered to roughly the $20,000 plus or minus range per ton.”

  • Submitted question (online): asked about LFP significance; CEO Cotton said the breakthrough is economic recovery of lithium while “recovering the iron phosphate into a reusable form,” and tied it to “the growing volume of LFP GigaFactory scrap.”

  • Submitted question (online): asked about liquidity/runway; CFO West said, “we ended the year with $10.8 million of cash, no long-term debt and a lower operating burn,” adding that “the capital raised during 2025 was about $20 million.”

  • Submitted question (online): asked how MOUs/LOI fit the core; CEO Cotton said partnerships are “about applying our core AquaRefining platform to new sources of critical minerals,” and called them “directionally aligned and not a distraction at all.”

  • Submitted question (online): asked about consolidation; CEO Cotton said consolidation is “a net positive,” stating, “as the weaker models fall away, we think that our position does become ... more differentiated,” and said the company’s process produces “0 sodium sulfate waste stream.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral to slightly positive, with Legg opening with “Congrats” and focusing on deal synergies, ABF equity implications, and timing.

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive and expansion-focused in prepared remarks, with repeated confidence language around readiness and differentiation; Cotton said, “build once, build right,” and framed LFP progress as “one of the most significant technical achievements.”

  • Compared with Q3, management repeated the “discipline” posture and continued to frame consolidation as beneficial, while Q4 added more detailed framing around Lion Energy/ABF integration and provided fuller-year financial context.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q4, Cotton more explicitly defined the first ARC’s narrowed initial scope (NMC and LFP black mass; outputs lithium carbonate, MHP, and iron phosphate) and added the facility sizing range of “10,000 to 60,000 metric tons,” while Q3 emphasized platform validation and commercial pathway expansion.

  • In Q4, West provided full-year financial detail (including year-end cash of $10.8 million and no long-term debt), while Q3 focused on quarter-end cash of $2.9 million and the then-recent $13 million post-quarter investment.

  • Q4 introduced new disclosed Lion Energy financing and subsequent additional $2 million subordinated interest; Q3 did not discuss the Lion financing amounts.

  • Analyst questioning shifted from Q3’s emphasis on “gating factors” for the first build and commissioning timing to Q4’s emphasis on Lion Energy/ABF strategic synergies, with additional investor-submitted questions covering site selection and feedstock economics.

Risks and Concerns

  • Cotton highlighted gating risks before construction: the company requires “contracted feedstock, committed offtake and project financing that is genuinely bankable.”

  • Cotton cited exposure to commodity and market cycles, noting that refining projects slowed when lithium carbonate prices “fell to around $8,000 a ton in 2024,” and framed improved economics with pricing “recovered to roughly the $20,000 plus or minus range per ton.”

  • West flagged an expected spend ramp: “we anticipate a measured increase in cash usage as we ramp engineering, process optimization and site readiness activities.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed 2025 as a year of tighter commercialization focus and balance sheet cleanup, highlighting a simplified first ARC centered on NMC and LFP black mass with three initial products, an ARC design range of 10,000 to 60,000 metric tons, and a commitment to not begin construction until feedstock, offtake, and “bankable” financing are secured. Executives also emphasized LFP processing at a 1-metric-ton scale and lithium carbonate quality progress, while positioning an in-progress Lion Energy transaction (including ABF equity exposure) as potentially expanding near-term revenue opportunities and downstream integration.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Jushi outlines Virginia adult-use timeline with licenses due by December 1, 2026 and sales expected January 1, 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Jushi Holdings (JUSHF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO James Cacioppo said Q4 revenue was $68.3 million and full-year revenue was $262.9 million, adding that results reflected “continued stabilization across our retail footprint,” with contributions from new stores and “enhanced product availability and quality driven by improved operational execution at our grower-processor facilities.”
  • CEO Cacioppo highlighted a refinancing completed March 27, 2026: “We completed the refinancing through the issuance of a $160 million first lien secured term loan due in 2029 with a 12.5% coupon structured as interest-only payments over the 36-month term,” and said it was done “without issuing any warrants or equity-linked securities,” adding it provided “$13 million of incremental liquidity” and a “single financial covenant requiring the maintenance of a minimum cash balance.”
  • CEO Cacioppo framed Virginia adult-use as a key upcoming catalyst and said the reconciled bill sent to the governor sets “applications expected to be released on or before September 1, 2026, and license issuance on or before December 1, 2026,” with “Retail sales ... expected to commence on January 1, 2027.”
  • CFO Michelle Mosier said the Q4 retail revenue increase was driven by “contributions from new stores in Ohio and strong sales performance from all our Virginia stores,” while noting Virginia growth was “primarily driven by increased units sold, while average selling prices remained relatively flat.”

Outlook

  • CFO Mosier guided 2026 capital expenditures: “we currently expect maintenance CapEx to be in the range of approximately $4 million to $5 million” and “2026 growth CapEx to be in the range of $5 million to $8 million,” for “total projected capital expenditures of $9 million to $13 million in 2026,” while noting Virginia adult-use would influence timing and that “the majority of construction-related capital spending [would] occur in 2027 rather than 2026.”
  • CEO Cacioppo described Virginia readiness as requiring “minimal incremental capital investment” for adult-use because “our manufacturing and retail infrastructure are currently prepared,” while also detailing capacity initiatives including adding flowering rooms and hydrocarbon extraction.
  • Compared with Q3’s Virginia expectations (management then anticipated adult-use sales could begin “between July 1 to October 1, 2026”), the Q4 call shifted to a legislative framework targeting January 1, 2027 retail sales and included a disclosed conversion fee structure.

Financial Results

  • The company reported Q4 revenue of $68.3 million and adjusted EBITDA of $13.9 million; CFO Mosier attributed adjusted EBITDA improvement in part to “some ERC credit income during this quarter of about $3 million.”
  • CFO Mosier reported Q4 retail revenue of $60.4 million and wholesale revenue of $7.9 million, and said wholesale growth was “led by Massachusetts and Ohio,” while Virginia wholesale declined as “wholesale partners continue to prioritize their own vertical sell-through.”
  • CFO Mosier reported Q4 gross profit of $28.6 million (41.9% of revenue) and said the margin improvement reflected “higher production volumes, improved product quality and stronger performance across our grower-processor facilities,” partially offset by “increased promotional activity.”
  • CFO Mosier reported Q4 interest expense of $10.4 million and Q4 net loss of $15.6 million, and said year-end cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash were $26.6 million.

Q&A

  • Frederico Gomes, ATB Cormark: Asked about sequential gross margin decline vs. Q3’s 46.7% and how that reconciled with adjusted EBITDA margin expansion; Founder, Chairman & CEO Cacioppo said Q3 had “a bulge of packaged goods” lowering unit costs and that in Q4 the company “pulled back in October and November,” plus December promotions; CFO Mosier said EBITDA improved with “about $3 million” of ERC income.
  • Frederico Gomes, ATB Cormark: Asked how much higher branded penetration can go and whether there is a target; CEO Cacioppo said they do not “really target it,” noting it runs “in the 60s, mid-60s in most of the markets,” with Ohio the main limiter due to supply, and tied future Ohio vertical expansion to margin improvement.
  • Luke Hannan, Canaccord Genuity: Asked how much of the $9 million to $13 million 2026 CapEx includes Virginia build-out and the potential size of adult-use readiness spending; CEO Cacioppo said “what we haven't planned in this year is $2 million to $3 million related to building out in warehouse,” discussed equipping additional rooms and adding hydrocarbon extraction, and said the larger warehouse expansion doubling canopy is “not in the budget.”
  • Luke Hannan, Canaccord Genuity: Asked about Virginia’s conversion fee and payment plan; CEO Cacioppo confirmed “$10 million,” and Co-Chief Strategy Director Trenton Woloveck said, “we get to propose a payment plan ... over 3 years,” with a first payment “by December 1 of this year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, with pointed questions on sequential gross margin decline and funding/capital timing for Virginia, including framing requests like “Can you just frame up to us also what the size of that spend could be?” (Luke Hannan, Canaccord Genuity).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and execution-focused in prepared remarks, emphasizing balance sheet actions and Virginia timing; in Q&A, management was explanatory on margin mechanics and pointed to discrete drivers like production pacing and holiday promotions.
  • Versus Q3, where management repeatedly emphasized optimism on refinancing and earlier Virginia timing, Q4 conveyed greater procedural specificity (license dates, conversion fee/payment structure) and framed refinancing as completed rather than pending.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The refinancing narrative shifted from Q3’s “we have not yet secured financing” (CFO Mosier) to Q4’s completed $160 million first-lien term loan due 2029, alongside management’s emphasis on “no dilution to shareholders” from warrants or equity-linked securities.
  • Virginia adult-use expectations moved from Q3’s estimated sales start “between July 1 to October 1, 2026” to Q4’s expectation that sales “commence on January 1, 2027,” with added details on dual-use conversion timing and a $10 million fee.
  • Q4 placed more emphasis on capex sequencing and conditionality (not budgeting the major Virginia warehouse build yet), while continuing Q3’s themes of operational improvements, yield/potency gains, and retail footprint optimization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited “pricing pressure” and “increased promotional activity” across markets, and said enforcement against “illicit and intoxicating hemp products remains uneven in certain regions.”
  • CFO Mosier noted Virginia wholesale softness tied to partners prioritizing vertical sell-through, and management repeatedly tied major capital plans to regulatory clarity, particularly in Virginia and Pennsylvania.
  • CEO Cacioppo described capital-market constraints indirectly through references to “cost of capital” (Ohio expansion gating) and pursuing “mortgage and other possible traditional financing options” for Virginia build-outs.

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q4 as continued stabilization with improved operational performance, and positioned the March 2026 refinancing as a balance-sheet and liquidity reset extending maturities to 2029 without warrants or equity-linked securities. The call’s central forward focus was Virginia adult-use: management highlighted a legislative pathway with applications by September 1, 2026, license issuance by December 1, 2026, a $10 million conversion fee with an operator-proposed payment plan over three years, and retail sales expected to begin January 1, 2027, while sequencing major expansion spending largely into 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sportsman's Warehouse forecasts 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $30M-$36M while targeting flat to +2% comps
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sportsman's Warehouse (SPWH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Paul Stone said results “exceeded our revised expectations,” adding that “we turned our sales trends positive in the back half of the quarter,” and that full-year comparable sales rose 1%, calling it “our first year of positive comps since 2020 and a meaningful milestone in our turnaround.”
  • Stone said Q4 started with pressure, noting “for several weeks prior and through the first week of December, sales softened” on “the government shutdown and weaker-than-expected Black Friday and Cyber Week performance,” and that the company “moved quickly to adjust our holiday strategy with a more promotional cadence to meet a value-driven consumer.”
  • Stone highlighted category performance, stating “hunting and shooting sports grew more than 5% with firearm units again outperforming adjusted NICS checks,” and said this “indicat[es] continued market share gains,” while also saying “camping and softlines remain challenged.”
  • Stone described initiatives for 2026, including “upgrading our loyalty rewards program,” “developing firearm solution bundling,” and “reinventing the omnichannel Fishing experience,” adding that for fishing “we believe we have about 1% share... and we have an ambitious omnichannel plan to double that share over the next 3 to 4 years.”
  • CFO Jennifer Fall Jung reported, “Adjusted EBITDA for the year was $27.5 million,” and said inventory ended the year “down $29.1 million or 8.5% year-over-year,” with “net debt of $90 million... and total liquidity of $107.8 million,” adding, “We also generated positive free cash flow.”

Outlook

  • CFO Jennifer Fall Jung guided, “We estimate same-store sales to be in the range of down 1% to up 2% over last year,” and “we expect adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $30 million to $36 million,” adding capex is expected to be “between $20 million and $25 million.”
  • Jung said store actions are planned: “we estimated we will be closing approximately 5 stores in the next 12 months,” and added, “we do not anticipate a material impact to this year's results” because “we expect these closures to happen after the holidays.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s tone, management moved from Q3 caution on a “challenging start to Q4” and revised full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance of $22 million to $26 million to Q4 commentary that results “exceeded our revised guidance following Q3” and issuance of 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $30 million to $36 million.

Financial Results

  • CFO Jennifer Fall Jung said Q4 net sales were $334.9 million and comparable store sales declined 1.8%, while gross margin was 28.4% versus 30.4% last year, and SG&A improved to 28.7% of net sales versus 29.4% last year.
  • Jung reported profitability metrics for Q4: “Net loss for the quarter was $21.7 million or $0.56 per diluted share,” while “adjusted net loss for the quarter was $3.9 million or negative $0.10 per diluted share,” and “adjusted EBITDA was $9.6 million.”
  • On category drivers in Q4, Jung said Hunting and Shooting Sports increased 6.2% and Fishing increased 3.2%, while noting that “excluding ice fishing, sales in this category were up over 11%,” and attributed pressure to “unseasonably warm weather in the Western U.S.”
  • On balance sheet and cash flow, Jung said year-end inventory was $312.9 million, “net debt of $90 million,” “total liquidity of $107.8 million,” and “we generated $8.9 million of free cash flow and used that cash to reduce debt,” adding, “Debt reduction remains our top capital allocation priority.”

Q&A

  • Matt Koranda, ROTH Capital: Asked if Q1 is comping positive and what categories are driving; CFO Jung replied that trends “started in January” and “continue through February and March,” driven by “really strong growth coming from firearms and ammunition,” adding, “we feel like we're coming out strong in Q1.”
  • Matt Koranda, ROTH Capital: Asked for the “building blocks” behind 2026 EBITDA improvement despite flattish comps and mix pressure; CFO Jung said Q1 margins are expected “to be down year-over-year” due to “penetration of firearms and ammunition,” while later quarters should be “flat to slightly positive,” with SG&A “flat to some slight leverage.”
  • Matt Koranda, ROTH Capital: Asked about free cash flow and inventory efficiency and store-closure impacts; CFO Jung said there is “opportunity to continue to find efficiency in inventory,” and closures are expected “after the holidays,” while inventory may be “transfer[red]” and seasonal goods liquidated; CEO Stone added the focus is “discipline in the inventory approach... to improve the turns.”
  • Anna Glaessgen, B. Riley Securities: Asked about risk if conflict extends and consumer headwinds; CFO Jung said “the health of the U.S. consumer is really the risk,” citing “fuel prices,” while also pointing to “the 250th anniversary of America” as an offset and said “there's a lot of variables.”
  • Anna Glaessgen, B. Riley Securities: Asked how hunt/ammo penetration affects longer-term margin recovery; CFO Jung said mix is contemplated, and highlighted efforts to “put our shoulder against fish” and “cleaning up the apparel business,” while CEO Stone emphasized “the bundling component” online to improve attachment and said fishing is “underpenetrated online.”
  • Mark Smith, Lake Street: Asked what drove Q4 gross margin pressure and whether tariffs were a factor; CFO Jung said it was “a combination of mix as well as promotional cadence,” and added, “there's not a ton of tariff impact in here.”
  • Mark Smith, Lake Street: Asked about Q1 margin outlook; CFO Jung said it is “a mix” and “heavily penetrated towards firearm and ammunition,” while CEO Stone said early-year fish volume is not enough “to be able to offset it.”
  • Mark Smith, Lake Street: Asked about store impairments and closures; CFO Jung said the targeted stores are “losing adjusted EBITDA stores,” and the company is exploring “renegotiate, get a subtenant... do a buyout,” while noting some impaired stores may “just let run off” as leases near expiry.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, focusing on durability of event-driven demand, consumer pressure, and margin structure, including questions about “potential risk as the conflict extends” (Anna Glaessgen, B. Riley Securities) and Q4 margin headwinds (Mark Smith, Lake Street).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive but measured, with CEO Stone stating, “we feel optimistic about our positioning,” while also cautioning “we remain measured as the U.S. consumer remains under pressure,” and CFO Jung repeatedly referencing mix-driven margin pressure and consumer sensitivity to fuel.
  • Versus the prior quarter, management shifted from Q3 emphasis on a “challenging start to Q4” and margin pressure from promotional intensity to Q4 messaging that results “exceeded our revised expectations,” while maintaining caution around the consumer and noting mix pressure from firearms/ammo.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The key narrative shifted from Q3’s acknowledgment of a mid-October slowdown and a revised full-year adjusted EBITDA range of $22 million to $26 million to Q4’s statement that results “exceeded our revised guidance following Q3,” alongside new 2026 guidance for comps and adjusted EBITDA.
  • Strategic emphasis in Q4 expanded from Q3 priorities (inventory precision, local relevance, personal protection, brand awareness) to concrete 2026 build plans: loyalty program overhaul, firearm “solution bundling,” and an omnichannel fishing reinvention, including the stated aim to “double” fishing share over “3 to 4 years.”
  • Analyst focus remained consistent across calls on promotions, margins, and demand trends, but Q4 questioning leaned more into forward margin building blocks, store closures, and the sustainability of externally influenced firearms/ammo demand.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management said “the U.S. consumer remains under pressure,” and cited “rising fuel costs and broader macro dynamics” as a headwind, with CFO Jung reiterating consumer health as “the risk.”
  • Management flagged mix and promotional pressures on profitability, with CFO Jung attributing Q4 gross margin decline to “category mix... increased promotional activity and lower sales in higher-margin categories,” and guiding that Q1 margins are expected to be down year-over-year due to firearms/ammo penetration.
  • Store portfolio actions include planned closures of “approximately 5 stores,” with CFO Jung noting they are “losing adjusted EBITDA stores” and that the company is pursuing lease renegotiations, subtenants, or buyouts.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q4 and full-year performance as a turnaround milestone, citing a return to positive full-year comps and better-than-revised results while acknowledging a pressured consumer and mix-driven margin headwinds. For 2026, the company guided to same-store sales of down 1% to up 2% and adjusted EBITDA of $30 million to $36 million, alongside plans to close about 5 underperforming stores after the holidays and to prioritize debt reduction. Strategic focus centered on scaling personal protection, improving attachment through firearm solution bundling, and accelerating fishing via an omnichannel rebuild and a stated plan to double category share over 3 to 4 years.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nike slides 9% after issuing soft guidance during its earnings call
data.description:
Nike (NKE) fell sharply in postmarket trading on Tuesday after issuing soft guidance on its earnings conference call.

The company expects sales to decline between 2% and 4% in FQ4 to miss the consensus expectation for an increase of 1.9%.  Notably, Nike (NKE) sees a 20% decline in revenue for the Greater China business.

CFO Matt Friend said the company recognizes that the environment has become increasingly dynamic and warned it could experience unplanned volatility due to the disruption in the Middle East, rising oil prices, and other factors that could impact either input costs or consumer behavior. Meanwhile, CFO Elliott Hill acknowledged the company's comeback is taking longer than he would like but thinks the current strategy will pay off in the long term.

Shares of Nike (NKE) moved down 9.1% in postmarket trading after moving 3.1% higher during the regular session.

data.title: Nike anticipates Q4 revenue down 2% to 4% as it targets finishing 'Win Now' actions by year-end
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NIKE, Inc. (NKE) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "Last quarter, we said we were in the middle innings of our comeback. Since then, we have continued to take meaningful actions to improve the health, quality and foundation of our business." (CEO, President & Director Elliott Hill)

  • "One of the most important actions we took this quarter was further removing unhealthy inventory of our classic footwear franchises from the marketplace. That created roughly a 5-point headwind to our reported results. It was intentional. It was necessary." (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • "NIKE Running up over 20% for the quarter." (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • "Our new Nike Mind platform with over 150 patents filed globally was the highlight of the quarter... The Mind 001 sold out in all geographies." (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • "We responded by doubling production of NIKE Mind over the next 2 seasons to meet demand from more than 2 million consumers who signed up for notify me on NIKE.com." (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • "You saw the early signs of those actions this quarter, and it is a critical step in our return to double-digit EBIT margins." (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • "For this quarter, revenues were flat on a reported basis and down 3% on a currency-neutral basis." (Executive VP & CFO Matthew Friend)

  • "Gross margins declined 130 basis points to 40.2% on a reported basis, primarily due to 300 basis points associated with higher tariffs in North America." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "Let me provide some additional context on the $230 million charge we incurred this quarter due to employee-related severance costs primarily in supply chain and technology." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

Outlook

  • "We expect revenues in Q4 to be down 2% to 4%." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We expect Greater China to be down approximately 20% in the fourth quarter, reflecting reduced sell-in... as well as accelerated actions to clean up the marketplace." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We expect sequential improvement in gross margin with Q4 down approximately 25 to 75 basis points, including 250 basis points due to higher tariffs in North America." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We expect Q4 SG&A dollars to be flat to down slightly." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We expect other expense net of interest income to be an expense of $15 million to $25 million in the fourth quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We will return to providing full year and long-term guidance at our Investor Day in the fall." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • Change from last quarter’s framing: in Q2, management emphasized a 90-day guidance approach ("we've been providing 90 days of guidance for the last 5 quarters") while in Q3 it added a broader view ("provide greater visibility to how we see the business trend from here through the end of this calendar year"). (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

Financial Results

  • Revenue of $11,229,428,660.0 vs. $11,229,428,660.0 (analysts estimate for fiscal Q3 2026). EPS of $0.35 vs. $0.28 (analysts estimate for fiscal Q3 2026).

  • "NIKE Direct was down 7% with NIKE Digital declining 9% and NIKE stores down 5%. Wholesale grew 1%." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "SG&A was up 2% on a reported basis versus the prior year due to employee severance charges we incurred in the quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "Our effective tax rate was 20%. Earnings per share was $0.35 and inventory decreased 1% versus the prior year with units down mid-single digits." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We continue to evaluate opportunities related to supply chain which could result in additional financial impacts in future quarters though we believe the actions taken this quarter will represent the largest financial impact." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

Q&A

  • Lorraine Maikis, BofA Securities: asked how EMEA issues are diagnosed and fixed; Executive VP & CFO Friend said EMEA is seeing "growth in performance" but "sell-through trends are not yet where we want them to be," pointing to macro/traffic pressures and promotional activity, and added it expects to exit Q4 with "elevated inventory in EMEA" while working through actions; CEO, President & Director Hill added "we do have a new leader in place" and said he has "tremendous confidence in César Garcia" and the focus is assortments, presentation, and sell-through with strategic partners.

  • Adrienne Yih-Tennant, Barclays: pressed on the calendar-year guidance shaping and what “flattish” earnings covers; Executive VP & CFO Friend said the guidance is meant to "provid[e] transparency... over the next 9 months" aligned to completing actions by year-end, with North America momentum offset by Greater China headwinds from reduced sell-in, and emphasized "we expect margins to inflect in Q2."

  • Simeon Siegel, Guggenheim: asked about DTC quality and severance in overhead; CEO, President & Director Hill said Nike is shifting from "a NIKE Direct first offense" to "a balanced and integrated marketplace"; Executive VP & CFO Friend pointed to North America digital improvement with better launches and "continued improvement in average retail discounts," plus "demand on the Nike App grow in Q3 in North America" and "positive growth in all channels... for the first time in 2 years."

  • Michael Binetti, Evercore ISI: asked about Q4 puts/takes in EMEA and strong EBIT despite revenue decline; Executive VP & CFO Friend said the change was Sportswear sell-through and guidance reflects careful marketplace management, also factoring "traffic" disruption in the Middle East.

  • Brooke Roach, Goldman Sachs: asked on stabilizing Sportswear and more reset in Classics; CEO, President & Director Hill said "the Air Force 1 and the AJ1, they stabilized this quarter" with "month-to-month improvement in full price realization" and noted "We're still stabilizing the Dunks," while citing strong launches (AJ 11 Gamma, AJ5 Wolf Grey, Air Max 95) with "high full price realization and really strong sell-through."

  • Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer: asked why the turnaround is taking longer (internal vs. external); CEO, President & Director Hill said "it's a little bit of both" and highlighted differing starting points by geography and marketplace structures.

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: asked about global sportswear backdrop and parsing "self-inflicted" headwinds and sell-through; CEO, President & Director Hill said sportswear remains "critical" and Nike is taking a "streets-up approach"; Executive VP & CFO Friend said to use the Q4 revenue range as a guide for the next two quarters and described continued cleaning actions in EMEA/APLA and managed sell-in in Greater China to drive "sequential improvement of sell-through rates."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone: slightly negative to pressing, with repeated probing on EMEA underperformance, guidance shape/“flattish” earnings, DTC quality vs. wholesale, and the duration/causes of the turnaround (e.g., "the process is taking longer than you initially expected"). (Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer)

  • Management tone: slightly positive but candid about near-term pressure, repeatedly emphasizing deliberate actions and time required ("we are not satisfied" and "parts of it are taking longer than I'd like") while pointing to confidence markers such as "I am confident" and "we remain confident" tied to stated priorities and timelines. (CEO, President & Director Hill)

  • Versus last quarter: management continued the "middle innings" framing but added more explicit near-term pressure drivers in Q3 (inventory removal headwind and severance), while Q2 leaned more heavily on "slightly better than we had anticipated" and momentum signals like wholesale growth and improving order books. (CEO, President & Director Hill)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance approach broadened in Q3: Q2 focused on Q3 guidance ("We expect Q3 revenues to be down low single digits") while Q3 added a calendar-year trend view ("provide greater visibility") and specific Q4 ranges ("down 2% to 4%") alongside a commitment to restore longer-term guidance at an Investor Day.

  • Turnaround actions shifted from leadership/structure emphasis to execution and cost reset: Q2 highlighted leadership changes ("all geographies will now report directly to me" and naming a COO) while Q3 emphasized additional marketplace cleanup ("further removing unhealthy inventory") and a cost-base reset including "the $230 million charge" and supply chain/technology actions.

  • Geographic narrative changed in EMEA: Q2 described EMEA as maintaining "a healthy marketplace" despite promotional activity, while Q3 described sharper pressure from Sportswear sell-through gaps, higher promotions, Middle East disruption, and an expectation to exit Q4 with "elevated inventory" in EMEA.

  • North America signals evolved: Q2 highlighted strong wholesale growth and digital premium repositioning; Q3 emphasized accelerating wholesale momentum, improving sell-through (not yet at target), and the first time in "2 years" of positive growth in all channels in North America.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Digital is still too promotional. Markdowns across the marketplace remain elevated." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We anticipate adding the fourth quarter with elevated inventory" in EMEA amid a "highly promotional marketplace" and "recent disruption in the Middle East." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We expect these actions will continue throughout fiscal '27 and remain a headwind to revenue growth" in Greater China as Nike reduces sell-in and cleans the marketplace. (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

  • "We also recognize that the environment around us has become increasingly dynamic, and could experience unplanned volatility due to the disruption in the Middle East, rising oil prices and other factors that could impact either input costs or consumer behavior." (Executive VP & CFO Friend)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q3 as a quarter of deliberate cleanup and restructuring: CEO Elliott Hill said Nike intentionally removed unhealthy classic footwear inventory, creating a roughly 5-point headwind, while pointing to faster-moving proof points including Running up over 20% and Nike Mind selling out. CFO Matt Friend reported flat revenue and $0.35 EPS, alongside a $230 million severance charge tied to resetting the cost base in supply chain and technology. For Q4, management guided revenue down 2% to 4% and said Greater China is expected to be down about 20%, while positioning gross margin to begin inflecting after tariff pressure eases and “Win Now” actions near completion by the end of the calendar year.

data.title: Edible Garden outlines RTD expansion with plans to be in market toward tail end of 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Edible Garden AG Incorporated (EDBL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • “2025 was a defining year for Enable Garden as we continue to build on our foundation and expand our long-term growth potential,” said Founder, CEO, President, Treasurer, Secretary & Chairman James Kras, describing “a deliberate strategy to grow beyond our core controlled environment agriculture platform into a broader innovation-driven consumer packaged goods business focusing on higher growth, higher margin opportunities.”
  • Kras said the company “secur[ed] new and expanded placements with key retail partners, including Kroger, Weis Markets, Safeway, The Fresh Market and Busch's, increasing our distribution to nearly 6,000 store locations,” and added that these efforts and “targeted investments in customer onboarding… resulted in incremental distribution of more than 700 additional retail locations.”
  • Kras highlighted category performance, citing “double-digit growth in cut herbs,” “continued strength in our vitamin and supplement portfolio,” and “significant growth in our condiment platform, supported by new customer wins such as Wakefern and Safeway.”
  • On strategy, Kras said, “Key next step in our strategy is expanding into the ready-to-drink, or RTD category,” adding that the company “announced the development of a state-of-the-art RTD manufacturing initiative at our Midwest facility” and “selected Tetra Pak… to plan, install and integrate proprietary processing capabilities.”
  • “Revenue for the 3 months ended December 31, 2025, was approximately $4.1 million compared to $3.9 million in the prior year period,” said Interim Chief Financial Officer Kostas Dafoulas, who also said Q4 cost pressures reflected onboarding investments and that “we expect the cost structure to normalize as those programs mature and volume increases.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide formal 2026 revenue or EPS guidance in this transcript.
  • Dafoulas provided margin and growth framework tied to portfolio mix, saying the core CEA business could “return to steady growth in the high single digits sort of range,” nutraceuticals showed “double-digit, 20%-ish range year-over-year” growth, and “blended margin kind of low double digits to mid-teens is a reasonable expectation going forward.”
  • For RTD, Dafoulas described “margins kind of in the 20% to 30% range” as “first expectations” while the company scopes inputs and project details.
  • On the RTD timeline, Kras said, “our plan is to really hopefully be out in the marketplace probably towards the tail end of 2027.”

Financial Results

  • Dafoulas reported Q4 revenue of “approximately $4.1 million” and said the quarter included “our first international CPG segment of Kick. Sports Nutrition to PriceSmart.”
  • Dafoulas said Q4 cost of goods sold was “approximately $5.3 million,” and gross profit was “approximately a $1.2 million loss,” attributing the quarter to “a deliberate decision to absorb elevated costs to secure a 2026 shelf space.”
  • Dafoulas reported Q4 SG&A of “approximately $4.6 million,” citing “depreciation and rent tied to the NaturalShrimp asset acquisition,” “higher legal and professional fees,” “capital markets activities,” and “higher compensation expenses,” and added that “a meaningful portion reflects nonrecurring or deal-related costs rather than ongoing run rate expense.”
  • For the full year, Dafoulas reported revenue of “approximately $12.8 million versus $13.9 million in 2024,” stating “the headline decline is largely a function of our strategic exit from floral and lettuce.”
  • Dafoulas reported full-year cost of goods sold of “approximately $13 million,” full-year gross profit of “approximately a loss of $0.2 million,” and said “gross margin recovery is a top priority for 2026.”
  • Dafoulas reported full-year SG&A of “approximately $15.3 million versus $11.6 million in 2024,” and said “stockholders' equity improved through the preferred stock issuance associated with the NaturalShrimp acquisition” while “total debt declined approximately $0.6 million year-over-year.”

Q&A

  • Jeremy Pearlman, Maxim Group: asked how to view margins shifting from fresh to CPG/RTD and the CPG vs. fresh breakdown into 2026; CEO Kras said shelf-stable RTD/CPG should have “much more of a robust margin” and “a much more stable business,” and CFO Dafoulas added blended margin expectations of “low double digits to mid-teens” with RTD “margins kind of in the 20% to 30% range.”
  • Jeremy Pearlman, Maxim Group: asked where the company will compete in RTD and whether it will be produced at the Midwest facility; CEO Kras said RTD will be “primarily in the protein segment,” spanning “sports nutrition, performance nutrition,” including “our own Kick” and “a GLP-1… under our Jealousy brand,” and also “private label,” adding, “our goal is… to sell out the plant in the next 90 days or so.”
  • Jeremy Pearlman, Maxim Group: asked about CapEx, build-out timeline, and scale for the Midwest facility; CEO Kras said, “I don't want to give any specific numbers,” but described “some real CapEx” and said the company is working on “incentives,” with a target to be “out in the marketplace… towards the tail end of 2027.”
  • Nick Pincus, Forest Capital: asked about sustainability of distribution gains to “nearly 6,000 locations”; CEO Kras said exits from “floral and lettuce” helped focus the business, cited “good same-store sales,” and said, “you're going to see more store count… across the whole business,” including RTD.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with questions focused on margin structure, revenue mix visibility, and RTD execution; Pearlman called RTD “a really great opportunity for the company” while pressing for CapEx and timelines.
  • Management: positive and expansion-focused in prepared remarks and Q&A; Kras called RTD “transformative for Edible Garden” and Dafoulas emphasized normalization and recovery, including “we expect the cost structure to normalize” and “gross margin recovery is a top priority for 2026.”
  • Versus Q3 2025, the current call leaned more heavily into RTD manufacturing plans and timeline language, while Q3 emphasized the shift toward shelf-stable CPG and retail footprint expansion.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The most notable shift from Q3 to Q4 was the move from discussing the NaturalShrimp/Midwest facility as an R&D and product hub to positioning it as an RTD manufacturing initiative with Tetra Pak integration and a stated target to be in market “towards the tail end of 2027.”
  • Q4 emphasized distribution scale (“nearly 6,000 store locations”) and onboarding-driven cost pressure, while Q3 emphasized that shelf-stable portfolio growth was “54% year-over-year in Q3” and detailed cash and net loss figures not repeated in the Q4 prepared financial remarks.
  • Q&A focus moved from Q3 questions about initial facility utilization and private label/margin mechanics to Q4 questions centered on RTD category focus, expected margins, and CapEx/timing.

Risks and Concerns

  • Dafoulas flagged near-term cost and margin pressure from onboarding, saying Q4 costs reflected onboarding “during a seasonally compressed period,” and gross profit was impacted by a “deliberate decision to absorb elevated costs to secure a 2026 shelf space.”
  • Kras declined to provide RTD CapEx specifics, saying, “I don't want to give any specific numbers,” while acknowledging the need to “buy machines and retrofit a building” and describing it as “some real CapEx.”
  • Dafoulas said part of the nutraceutical trade-off is margin, noting co-manufacturing means “the margins are not as rich as if we were to do it ourselves.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q4 2025 as a quarter of expanded national distribution and deliberate, front-loaded onboarding investment that pressured gross profit, while reiterating that margin recovery is a stated 2026 priority. The call’s central strategic update was the RTD initiative at the Midwest facility with Tetra Pak integration, with management positioning RTD and broader shelf-stable categories as the path to a more scalable, higher-margin mix and targeting commercial presence “towards the tail end of 2027.”
